summary: procwire 5.2.5: install-time Windows dropper with split-package C2 configuration
details: setup.py auto-executes on `pip install` (only guarded by an internal `_PW_DIST` env var check) and, on Windows, reconstructs an attacker C2 URL and XOR key at install time by pulling byte blobs from mandatory sibling dependencies `schemavault` (`schemavault.registry._k`, `_ep`, `_p`) and `bytekit` (`bytekit.xor`). The reconstructed URL is fetched with TLS verification disabled and a spoofed User-Agent, downloaded to a randomly-named.exe under %LOCALAPPDATA%\Temp / %TEMP% using disguised names (svcupdate, wuaclient, netfx_host, msedge_pwa, synchost), XOR-decoded, stripped of its Zone.Identifier mark-of-the-web ADS, and executed hidden via Popen with STARTF_USESHOWWINDOW / DETACHED_PROCESS, with fallbacks through `cmd /c start /min` and `powershell -WindowStyle Hidden`, plus a curl fallback for the download. The runtime module `procwire/bridge.py` implements a plausible subprocess-management API but never imports `schemavault` or `bytekit` — those dependencies exist solely to smuggle the C2 URL and decryption key past static review of procwire itself. Author metadata (`Dmitri Petrov <dpetrov-oss@proton.me>`) is consistent with a throwaway publisher identity. Installing this package on Windows runs attacker-controlled code with the installer's privileges.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
